subject: Anatomy
stem: A medical student is studying branchial arch derivatives. Which of the following bones is derived from the 1st pharyngeal arch (Meckel's cartilage)?
explanation: The malleus and incus are derived from Meckel's cartilage (1st pharyngeal arch), along with the anterior ligament of malleus and sphenomandibular ligament. The stapes is from the 2nd arch (Reichert's cartilage). The greater horn of hyoid is from the 3rd arch. Arytenoid cartilage is from the 6th arch. Note: The mandible ossifies AROUND Meckel's cartilage but is NOT derived from it (intramembranous ossification).
highYield: Malleus and incus come from 1st arch (Meckel's cartilage); stapes from 2nd arch (Reichert's cartilage)

subject: Anatomy
stem: Tensor tympani and tensor veli palatini muscles share the same nerve supply. Which cranial nerve innervates both these muscles?
explanation: Both tensor tympani and tensor veli palatini are innervated by CN V3 (mandibular nerve), as they are 1st arch derivatives. All 1st arch muscles are innervated by CN V3 — this includes muscles of mastication (temporalis, masseter, medial and lateral pterygoids), mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, tensor tympani, and tensor veli palatini. CN VII innervates 2nd arch muscles. CN IX innervates only stylopharyngeus.
highYield: All 1st arch muscles are innervated by CN V3, including tensor tympani and tensor veli palatini

subject: Anatomy
stem: Which of the following is the ONLY muscle derived from the 3rd pharyngeal arch?
explanation: Stylopharyngeus is the only muscle derived from the 3rd pharyngeal arch and is uniquely innervated by CN IX (glossopharyngeal nerve) — making it the sole muscle supplied by CN IX. Stylohyoid and posterior belly of digastric are 2nd arch (CN VII) muscles. Superior pharyngeal constrictor is a 4th arch derivative (CN X, superior laryngeal branch). This is a classic NEET PG examiner favorite.
highYield: Stylopharyngeus is the ONLY 3rd arch muscle, innervated solely by CN IX

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient has paralysis of all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except cricothyroid. Which pharyngeal arch structure failure best explains this finding?
explanation: The 6th pharyngeal arch gives rise to remaining laryngeal cartilages (arytenoid, corniculate, cuneiform) and all intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT cricothyroid, innervated by the recurrent laryngeal nerve (branch of CN X). Cricothyroid is from the 4th arch, innervated by the superior laryngeal nerve. So a 6th arch nerve lesion (recurrent laryngeal nerve) spares cricothyroid but paralyzes all other intrinsic laryngeal muscles. Note: 5th arch regresses completely and has no derivatives.
highYield: Recurrent laryngeal nerve (6th arch) supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT cricothyroid (4th arch, superior laryngeal nerve)

subject: Anatomy
stem: During embryological development, which pharyngeal pouch gives rise to the INFERIOR parathyroid gland, and why does it end up inferior despite originating from a higher pouch?
options: 3rd pouch, because it migrates further inferiorly than the 4th pouch
4th pouch, because it descends with the thyroid
1st pouch, because it remains attached to the ear
explanation: The 3rd pharyngeal pouch gives rise to the inferior parathyroid gland AND the thymus. Despite being higher in origin than the 4th pouch, the 3rd pouch migrates further inferiorly (descends with the thymus), ending up below the 4th pouch derivative. The 4th pouch gives rise to the superior parathyroid. This is a classic exam paradox: 3rd pouch = INFERIOR parathyroid. The 4th pouch also gives rise to the ultimobranchial body (parafollicular C-cells of thyroid).
highYield: 3rd pouch = inferior parathyroid + thymus (migrates further, hence inferior); 4th pouch = superior parathyroid

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 25-year-old presents with a neck swelling along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid that transilluminates. This is most likely a remnant of which embryological structure?
explanation: A persistent 2nd branchial cleft forms a lateral neck cyst along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid. It is the most common branchial cleft anomaly. Key distinguishing features: lateral location, anterior to SCM, and it transilluminates. 1st branchial cleft remnants appear near the ear or parotid region. Thyroglossal duct cysts are midline (not lateral) and move with swallowing and tongue protrusion — they are NOT branchial cleft cysts. 3rd pharyngeal pouch gives parathyroid and thymus.
highYield: Lateral neck cyst at anterior SCM border = 2nd branchial cleft remnant (most common branchial cleft anomaly)

subject: Anatomy
stem: Waldeyer's lymphatic ring consists of several tonsils arranged around the pharynx. What is the total number of distinct tonsil masses comprising this ring?
explanation: Waldeyer's ring consists of 6 masses: 1 pharyngeal tonsil (adenoid, roof of nasopharynx) + 2 tubal tonsils (Gerlach tonsils, posterior to each Eustachian tube opening) + 2 palatine tonsils (between anterior and posterior pillars) + 1 lingual tonsil (base of tongue) = total 6. Note that Waldeyer's ring is NOT a complete, continuous ring — it is noncontinuous. This is a frequently tested anatomical fact.
highYield: Waldeyer's ring = 6 masses total: 1 pharyngeal + 2 tubal + 2 palatine + 1 lingual tonsil

subject: Anatomy
stem: During a tonsillectomy, a surgeon accidentally ruptures a vessel lateral to the palatine tonsil, causing profuse uncontrollable hemorrhage. Which vessel is most likely at risk during tonsillectomy if dissection goes too deep?
explanation: While the tonsillar branch of the facial artery is the MAIN blood supply to the palatine tonsil, the most dangerous vessel during tonsillectomy is the external carotid artery. It lies laterally, and if dissection goes too deep, it can be injured, causing life-threatening hemorrhage. The lesser palatine and ascending pharyngeal supply the upper pole. The paratonsillar vein drains the tonsil. The facial artery's tonsillar branch supplies the lower pole (main supply), but ECA itself is the dangerous vessel.
highYield: Most dangerous vessel during tonsillectomy = external carotid artery (lateral, at risk with deep dissection)

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 28-year-old construction worker presents after falling asleep with his arm draped over a park bench. On examination, he has wrist drop and finger drop but intact elbow extension. Which level of radial nerve injury does this pattern suggest?
options: At the spiral groove of humerus (Saturday night palsy)
At the posterior interosseous nerve level
explanation: Injury at the spiral groove (midshaft humeral fracture or 'Saturday night palsy') causes wrist drop + finger drop with the TRICEPS SPARED because the branch to triceps arises above the spiral groove. This distinguishes it from axillary injury (crutch palsy), where triceps is also paralyzed (loss of elbow extension). Brachioradialis and supinator are also lost at the spiral groove level. Sensation is lost over the anatomical snuffbox (superficial branch). Intact triceps points to spiral groove injury.
highYield: Radial nerve at spiral groove: wrist drop + finger drop, triceps SPARED (branch to triceps exits above spiral groove)

subject: Anatomy
stem: An image shows a patient with wrist drop, inability to extend fingers, and lost sensation over the posterior arm and forearm, with paralysis of elbow extension. Which nerve injury at which level is shown?
explanation: Radial nerve injury at the axilla (crutch palsy) causes all features of spiral groove injury PLUS triceps paralysis (loss of elbow extension). Sensation is lost over posterior arm, posterior forearm, and dorsum of hand (lateral 3.5 fingers). This differs from spiral groove injury where triceps is spared. The clinical scenario of lost elbow extension distinguishes axillary from spiral groove radial nerve injury. Crutches improperly used cause axillary compression.
highYield: Radial nerve at axilla: wrist drop + finger drop + triceps paralysis (elbow extension lost) — distinguishes from spiral groove

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 45-year-old diabetic presents with claw hand affecting only the ring and little fingers. The hand exam reveals loss of sensation over the medial 1.5 digits and weakness of all interossei. Which nerve lesion at which level explains this?
options: Ulnar nerve at medial epicondyle (elbow level)
explanation: Ulnar nerve injury at the medial epicondyle (cubital tunnel syndrome) causes claw hand affecting ring and little fingers (4th and 5th digits), due to loss of lumbricals 3 and 4 (intrinsic minus) and interossei — unopposed FDP causes IP joint flexion. At elbow level, FCU and FDP to ring/little fingers are ALSO paralyzed. All interossei (dorsal and palmar), hypothenar muscles, adductor pollicis, and medial 2 lumbricals are lost. Sensation lost over medial 1.5 digits and medial palm.
highYield: Ulnar nerve at elbow: claw hand (ring + little), FCU and FDP also paralyzed; at wrist only intrinsic hand muscles affected

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient with a high ulnar nerve lesion at the elbow shows LESS clawing than expected compared to a patient with a low ulnar nerve lesion at the wrist. Which mechanism best explains this 'ulnar paradox'?
options: High lesion spares the interossei so extension at IP joints is preserved
High lesion also paralyzes FDP to 4th and 5th fingers, reducing the flexion deformity
High lesion damages the radial nerve simultaneously, causing wrist extension
High lesion spares FCU, counteracting the claw tendency
explanation: The ulnar paradox states that a HIGH ulnar nerve lesion (at elbow) produces LESS clawing than a LOW lesion (at wrist). At the elbow level, FDP to ring and little fingers is ALSO paralyzed (no flexor force at IP joints) — so even though lumbricals 3+4 and interossei are lost, the opposing flexor (FDP) is also gone, reducing the clawing deformity. At the wrist (low lesion), FDP is intact but lumbricals and interossei are lost, resulting in more pronounced clawing. Hence: higher lesion, milder claw.
highYield: Ulnar paradox: high lesion = less claw because FDP to 4th/5th also paralyzed (no flexion deformity)

subject: Anatomy
stem: A child presents after a supracondylar fracture of the humerus with inability to pronate the forearm and a 'benediction hand' deformity. Which nerve is injured and at what level?
explanation: Supracondylar fracture in children most commonly injures the median nerve at the elbow level. This causes loss of pronation, wrist flexion (FCR), all FDS, lateral FDP (to index and middle), and FPL. Benediction hand occurs because index and middle fingers cannot flex (they stay extended when making a fist) while ring and little fingers flex via intact ulnar FDP. The anterior interosseous nerve (branch of median) at elbow level causes isolated FPL and lateral FDP paralysis only. The full median nerve injury at elbow gives the complete benediction hand.
highYield: Median nerve at elbow (supracondylar fracture): benediction hand — index + middle stay extended, ring + little flex via ulnar FDP

subject: Anatomy
topic: Nerve Lesions - Median Nerve at Wrist
stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with thenar muscle wasting, inability to oppose the thumb, and loss of sensation over the lateral 3.5 digits on the palmar side. FCR and FDS are intact. Which condition and muscle group is involved?
options: Ulnar nerve lesion at Guyon's canal — hypothenar muscles
Median nerve at carpal tunnel — LOAF muscles
Radial nerve at elbow — wrist extensors
Median nerve at elbow — all forearm flexors
explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve compression at wrist) causes ape hand (simian hand) — thenar wasting and inability to oppose the thumb — due to loss of LOAF muscles: Lumbricals 1+2, Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, Flexor pollicis brevis (superficial head). Sensation lost over lateral 3.5 digits palmar side and dorsal tips. Palmaris longus, FCR, and FDS are all SPARED because their branches arise above the wrist. FCR intact confirms wrist-level lesion, not elbow-level.
highYield: Median nerve at wrist (CTS): ape hand = LOAF muscle loss; FCR and FDS spared (branch above wrist)

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient sustains a fracture at the neck of the fibula in a road traffic accident. On examination, he has foot drop and cannot evert the foot, with sensory loss over the dorsum of foot and lateral leg. Which nerve is most likely injured?
options: Common peroneal nerve at neck of fibula
explanation: Common peroneal nerve injury at the neck of the fibula (most common lower limb nerve injury) causes foot drop with loss of dorsiflexion (deep peroneal branch — tibialis anterior, EHL, EDL) and loss of eversion (superficial peroneal branch — peroneus longus and brevis). Sensory loss over dorsum of foot and lateral leg. High stepping gait compensates for foot drop. First web space sensation is carried by the deep peroneal nerve. Tibial nerve injury causes loss of plantarflexion and toe flexion.
highYield: Common peroneal nerve at fibular neck: foot drop + loss of eversion + dorsum foot sensory loss; most common lower limb nerve injury

subject: Anatomy
stem: A patient develops inability to extend the knee, absent knee jerk, and loss of sensation over the anterior thigh and medial aspect of the leg following an inguinal hernia repair. Which nerve is most likely damaged?
options: Lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh
explanation: Femoral nerve injury in the inguinal region causes: loss of knee extension (quadriceps paralysis), absent knee jerk, impaired hip flexion (iliacus), and sensory loss over anterior thigh and medial leg and foot (via saphenous nerve, the terminal sensory branch of femoral nerve). The patient cannot climb stairs or extend the knee. Obturator nerve injury causes loss of thigh adduction with sensory loss over medial thigh. Lateral cutaneous nerve injury causes meralgia paresthetica (lateral thigh sensory loss only). Femoral nerve is at risk in inguinal hernia repair.
highYield: Femoral nerve injury: no knee extension + absent knee jerk + medial leg sensory loss (saphenous nerve) + no stair climbing

subject: Anatomy
stem: Which of the following correctly describes the branching pattern of the ulnar nerve in the forearm?
options: Ulnar nerve gives branches in the axilla to FCU
Ulnar nerve gives no branches above the elbow; it supplies FCU and medial half of FDP in the forearm
Ulnar nerve gives dorsal cutaneous branch at the wrist level before Guyon's canal
Ulnar nerve innervates all 4 lumbricals in the hand
explanation: A critical exam fact: the ulnar nerve gives NO branches above the elbow. In the forearm, it supplies FCU and the medial half of FDP (to ring and little fingers). In the hand, it supplies: all 4 dorsal interossei, all 3 palmar interossei, medial 2 lumbricals (3rd and 4th), adductor pollicis, deep head of FPB, and all hypothenar muscles. The dorsal cutaneous branch of ulnar nerve arises about 5 cm proximal to the wrist (before Guyon's canal). Lumbricals 1 and 2 are median nerve; 3 and 4 are ulnar nerve.
highYield: Ulnar nerve gives NO branches above the elbow — critical anatomical fact for nerve lesion MCQs

subject: Physiology
stem: On a Wiggers diagram, a student marks a notch on the aortic pressure curve as the point of aortic valve opening. The examiner corrects him. What does this dicrotic notch actually represent?
options: Aortic valve opening — start of ventricular ejection
Aortic valve closure — start of isovolumetric relaxation
Mitral valve closure — start of isovolumetric contraction
Aortic valve closure — end of systole and start of diastole
explanation: The dicrotic notch (incisura) on the aortic pressure curve marks AORTIC VALVE CLOSURE, NOT opening. It signals the beginning of isovolumetric relaxation (IVR) and the end of systole. Aortic valve opening occurs when LV pressure rises to approximately 80 mmHg (diastolic aortic pressure) — this is a pressure crossover, not a notch. S2 coincides with aortic and pulmonary valve closure (producing the dicrotic notch on the respective pressure curves). This is a classic Wiggers diagram MCQ trap.
highYield: Dicrotic notch = aortic valve CLOSURE (start of IVR), NOT aortic valve opening — the most common Wiggers diagram trap

subject: Physiology
stem: On auscultation, a patient has a split S2 that widens during inspiration and narrows during expiration. This is called physiological splitting. Which sequence of valve closures during inspiration explains this finding?
options: Mitral closes before tricuspid during inspiration
Aortic closes before pulmonary during expiration, gap widens during inspiration
Pulmonary closure is delayed during inspiration due to increased RV filling, widening the A2-P2 gap
Aortic closure is advanced during inspiration, causing the split
explanation: Physiological splitting of S2 is due to delayed pulmonary valve closure during inspiration. During inspiration, increased venous return to the right heart prolongs RV ejection time, delaying pulmonary valve closure (P2). Simultaneously, increased pulmonary vascular capacitance reduces LV filling slightly, slightly advancing aortic closure (A2). The net result is a wider A2-P2 gap during inspiration. S2 = semilunar valve closure (aortic precedes pulmonary normally). Reversed splitting (P2 before A2) occurs in LBBB and aortic stenosis.
highYield: Physiological S2 split: inspiration delays P2 (increased RV filling) → A2-P2 gap widens; reverses in LBBB/AS

subject: Physiology
stem: A 65-year-old hypertensive patient shows prolonged isovolumetric relaxation time (IVRT) on echocardiography. This is the earliest sign of which cardiac dysfunction?
options: Aortic regurgitation — early closure of aortic valve
Mitral stenosis — delayed mitral valve opening
explanation: Prolonged isovolumetric relaxation time (IVRT) is the earliest sign of diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation), commonly seen in hypertensive heart disease. IVRT begins at S2 (aortic valve closure) and ends when LV pressure falls below LA pressure and the mitral valve opens. Normally ~70 ms. When the LV relaxes slowly (as in LVH from hypertension), IVRT is prolonged — this precedes any change in ejection fraction (systolic function may remain normal). Diastolic dysfunction with preserved EF is now called HFpEF.
highYield: Prolonged IVRT = earliest sign of diastolic dysfunction (impaired relaxation) in hypertensive heart disease

subject: Physiology
stem: A researcher wants the most accurate measurement of GFR in a clinical study. Which substance fulfills the criteria of being freely filtered, neither secreted nor reabsorbed, and not metabolized by the kidney?
explanation: Inulin is the gold standard for GFR measurement (normal = 125 mL/min = 180 L/day filtered). It fulfills all ideal GFR marker criteria: freely filtered at glomerulus, neither secreted nor reabsorbed by tubules, not metabolized. Creatinine slightly overestimates GFR (~10%) because it is minimally secreted by the proximal tubule. PAH clearance measures effective renal plasma flow (RPF = ~650 mL/min), not GFR. Urea is partially reabsorbed (passive), so it underestimates GFR. Clearance hierarchy: PAH > Inulin > Creatinine > Urea.
highYield: Inulin = gold standard GFR marker; freely filtered, not secreted, not reabsorbed, not metabolized; GFR = 125 mL/min

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient's plasma glucose is 250 mg/dL. Glucose begins appearing in the urine above which plasma glucose threshold, and what is the underlying tubular mechanism involved?
options: Glucose appears above 100 mg/dL due to saturation of SGLT1
Glucose appears above 180 mg/dL when tubular Tm for glucose (~375 mg/min) is exceeded
Glucose appears above 300 mg/dL due to glucosuria in diabetes insipidus
Glucose appears above 250 mg/dL when GFR exceeds 125 mL/min
explanation: The renal threshold for glucose is approximately 180 mg/dL. At this plasma level, the filtered load of glucose begins to exceed the tubular maximum (Tm) for glucose reabsorption (~375 mg/min in males). Below Tm, all filtered glucose is reabsorbed by SGLT2 (proximal tubule, S1/S2 segments) and SGLT1 (S3). The splay phenomenon explains why glucosuria appears gradually before the sharp Tm — due to nephron heterogeneity. Glucose clearance becomes positive above renal threshold. In diabetes mellitus, persistent glucosuria occurs when plasma glucose consistently exceeds 180 mg/dL.
highYield: Renal threshold for glucose = 180 mg/dL; Tm for glucose = ~375 mg/min; splay accounts for gradual onset of glucosuria

subject: Physiology
stem: A patient undergoes assessment of pancreatic exocrine function. Which hormone, secreted in response to duodenal acidification, primarily stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion and acts as the physiological antacid?
explanation: Secretin, secreted by S cells in the duodenum, is released in response to duodenal acid (pH < 4.5) and fatty acids. Its primary action is stimulation of pancreatic bicarbonate secretion, which neutralizes acid entering the duodenum — making secretin the 'physiological antacid.' It also inhibits gastric acid secretion. CCK (from I cells) stimulates pancreatic enzyme secretion and gallbladder contraction. Gastrin (from G cells in antrum) stimulates HCl secretion. GIP potentiates insulin release (incretin effect). In Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, secretin paradoxically increases gastrin.
highYield: Secretin from S cells: stimulated by duodenal acid (pH <4.5) → pancreatic bicarbonate secretion; the physiological antacid

subject: Physiology
stem: Erythromycin is being prescribed as a prokinetic agent for a patient with gastroparesis. What is the pharmacological basis of its prokinetic action?
options: It is a GIP receptor agonist stimulating gastric motility
It is a motilin receptor agonist that mimics the interdigestive MMC
It inhibits secretin release, removing the braking effect on gastric emptying
It activates 5-HT4 receptors in the gut wall directly
explanation: Erythromycin acts as a motilin receptor agonist. Motilin is secreted by Mo cells in the upper small intestine during the FASTING state and initiates the Migrating Motor Complex (MMC), which acts as the 'intestinal housekeeper' by clearing undigested contents. Erythromycin binds motilin receptors and mimics this effect, enhancing gastric emptying — hence its use as a prokinetic in gastroparesis and post-operative ileus. Motilin is suppressed postprandially and in pregnancy (explaining GI hypomotility in pregnancy). This mechanism is distinct from 5-HT4 agonists (like metoclopramide).
highYield: Erythromycin prokinetic action = motilin receptor agonist; motilin initiates MMC (intestinal housekeeper) during fasting

subject: Physiology
stem: A polysomnography report states: 'EEG shows high-amplitude delta waves (<4 Hz) with >75 microvolts amplitude. No eye movements. Muscle tone present.' Which sleep stage is shown, and which clinical events characteristically occur during this stage?
options: REM sleep — REM behavior disorder and vivid dreams
Stage N2 — sleep spindles and K complexes
Stage N3 (Slow Wave Sleep) — sleepwalking and night terrors
Stage N1 — hypnic jerks and theta waves
explanation: Delta waves (<4 Hz, >75 microvolts) with no eye movements and preserved muscle tone characterize Stage N3 (Slow Wave Sleep / SWS). SWS is associated with: GH release (major peak), physical restoration, immune function, and parasomnias including sleepwalking (somnambulism) and night terrors (pavor nocturnus) — the patient has no memory of events. Stage N2 shows sleep spindles (12-14 Hz) and K complexes. REM shows low-voltage mixed-frequency EEG with muscle atonia and vivid dreams. N1 shows theta waves (4-7 Hz). Most SWS occurs in the first third of the night.
highYield: N3 (SWS) = delta waves (<4 Hz, >75 uV); sleepwalking + night terrors + GH release occur here

subject: Physiology
stem: A 22-year-old medical student suddenly falls to the ground laughing at a joke and cannot move for a few seconds. He also reports falling asleep during morning lectures despite adequate night sleep. Which neurotransmitter deficiency and hypothalamic nucleus is responsible?
options: Orexin/hypocretin deficiency from lateral hypothalamus neurons
GABA deficiency from ventrolateral preoptic nucleus
explanation: Narcolepsy with cataplexy is caused by loss of orexin (hypocretin) neurons in the lateral hypothalamus, leading to uncontrolled REM intrusion into wakefulness. The tetrad of narcolepsy: excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy (sudden bilateral muscle weakness triggered by strong emotion — as in this case with laughter), sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic/hypnopompic hallucinations. REM sleep neurochemistry: ACh drives REM, while serotonin and norepinephrine (both silent during REM) normally suppress it. Orexin normally maintains wakefulness by activating noradrenergic and serotonergic systems.
highYield: Narcolepsy = orexin/hypocretin deficiency (lateral hypothalamus); cataplexy triggered by emotion = pathognomonic

subject: Physiology
stem: A Nobel Prize in 2021 was awarded for the discovery of temperature and touch receptors. Which TRP channel is activated by noxious heat (>43°C), low pH, and capsaicin from chili peppers?
options: TRPV1 — noxious heat >43°C, low pH, and capsaicin
Piezo1 — mechanical stretch in red blood cells
explanation: TRPV1 (Transient Receptor Potential Vanilloid 1) is the capsaicin receptor discovered by David Julius (Nobel 2021, jointly with Ardem Patapoutian). It is activated by noxious heat (>43°C), low pH, and capsaicin. TRPM8 is activated by cool temperatures (<25°C) and menthol — this is why menthol feels cool. TRPV2 has a higher threshold (>52°C) and is capsaicin-insensitive. Piezo channels (Piezo1 and Piezo2, discovered by Patapoutian) are mechanosensors — Piezo2 mediates touch and proprioception. This Nobel discovery explains the molecular basis of pain and temperature sensation.
highYield: TRPV1 = capsaicin receptor; activated by noxious heat (>43°C), low pH, capsaicin; Nobel 2021 (Julius & Patapoutian)

subject: Biochemistry
stem: The 2024 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for the discovery of microRNA. Which enzyme cleaves the pri-miRNA in the nucleus to produce pre-miRNA, and which enzyme performs the final cytoplasmic cleavage?
options: Dicer in nucleus; Drosha in cytoplasm
Drosha/DGCR8 complex in nucleus; Dicer in cytoplasm
Ago2 in nucleus; Exportin-5 in cytoplasm
RNase III in nucleus; RISC complex in cytoplasm
explanation: miRNA biogenesis pathway: (1) RNA Pol II transcribes pri-miRNA in the nucleus; (2) Drosha/DGCR8 complex cleaves pri-miRNA to pre-miRNA (hairpin ~70 nt) in the nucleus; (3) Exportin-5 exports pre-miRNA to cytoplasm; (4) Dicer cleaves pre-miRNA to mature miRNA duplex (~22 nt) in cytoplasm; (5) One strand loads into RISC with Ago2, which either cleaves target mRNA (perfect complementarity, siRNA-like) or represses translation (imperfect complementarity, most miRNAs). Nobel 2024 awarded to Victor Ambros and Gary Ruvkun. Ago2 is the catalytic 'slicer' in RISC.
highYield: miRNA: Drosha/DGCR8 (nucleus) → Exportin-5 → Dicer (cytoplasm) → RISC with Ago2; Nobel 2024 Ambros & Ruvkun

subject: Biochemistry
stem: The 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded to Kariko and Weissman for their discovery that enabled mRNA vaccines. What key modification did they introduce into synthetic mRNA to reduce immune activation and increase translational efficiency?
options: Poly-A tail elongation to increase mRNA stability
Codon optimization to match human tRNA pool
explanation: Kariko and Weissman discovered that substituting pseudouridine (psi-U) for uridine in synthetic mRNA dramatically reduces innate immune activation (via reduced TLR7 and TLR8 signaling) and increases translational efficiency. Normal synthetic mRNA is recognized as foreign by innate immune receptors, triggering inflammatory responses and reducing protein output. Pseudouridine substitution allowed mRNA to evade immune detection — this was the key breakthrough enabling COVID-19 mRNA vaccines (Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna). While 5' cap and poly-A tail are important for stability, pseudouridine modification was the Nobel-winning innovation.
highYield: Pseudouridine substitution in mRNA = reduces TLR7/TLR8 immune activation + increases translation; Nobel 2023 basis of COVID mRNA vaccines

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Enzyme A has a Km of 0.1 mM and Enzyme B has a Km of 10 mM for the same substrate. Which statement correctly interprets these values?
options: Enzyme A has lower affinity for substrate than Enzyme B
Enzyme A has higher affinity for substrate (lower Km = higher affinity)
Km does not reflect enzyme-substrate affinity
Enzyme B reaches Vmax at lower substrate concentration
explanation: Km (Michaelis constant) = substrate concentration at half-maximal velocity (Vmax/2). LOW Km = HIGH affinity (enzyme reaches half-maximal rate at a lower substrate concentration). HIGH Km = LOW affinity (needs more substrate to reach half-maximal rate). Enzyme A (Km = 0.1 mM) has much higher affinity than Enzyme B (Km = 10 mM). At very high substrate concentrations [S] >> Km, the reaction becomes zero-order (rate equals Vmax, enzyme fully saturated). This is fundamental enzyme kinetics — a NEET PG staple.
highYield: Low Km = high affinity; High Km = low affinity; Km = [S] at Vmax/2 in Michaelis-Menten kinetics

subject: Biochemistry
stem: An inhibitor increases the Km of an enzyme without changing its Vmax. Adding excess substrate reverses the inhibition. On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, the lines for inhibited and uninhibited enzyme intersect on the y-axis. What type of inhibition is this?
explanation: Competitive inhibition: the inhibitor competes with substrate for the active site. Features: increased Km (apparent), unchanged Vmax (can be overcome by excess substrate), reversible. On Lineweaver-Burk plot, lines intersect on the Y-AXIS (same Vmax = same y-intercept, but different X-intercepts reflecting different Km). Classic example: malonate inhibits succinate dehydrogenase (structural analog of succinate). Non-competitive: Vmax decreased, Km unchanged, lines intersect on X-axis. Uncompetitive: both Km and Vmax decrease proportionally, lines are parallel on Lineweaver-Burk.
highYield: Competitive inhibition: Km increases, Vmax unchanged; Lineweaver-Burk lines intersect on Y-axis; reversed by excess substrate

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A Lineweaver-Burk plot shows two lines — uninhibited and inhibited — that are PARALLEL to each other (no intersection). Both Km and Vmax are decreased proportionally. What type of inhibition does this pattern represent?
explanation: Uncompetitive inhibition produces parallel lines on the Lineweaver-Burk (double reciprocal) plot — both Km and Vmax decrease proportionally. The uncompetitive inhibitor binds ONLY the enzyme-substrate (ES) complex, not the free enzyme. By removing ES complex from the reaction, it appears to both reduce apparent Km (increased affinity paradox) and reduce Vmax. Parallel lines on Lineweaver-Burk are the hallmark. Non-competitive: Vmax decreases, Km unchanged, lines intersect on X-axis. Competitive: Km increases, Vmax unchanged, lines intersect on Y-axis. Mixed inhibition: lines intersect neither on X nor Y axis.
highYield: Uncompetitive inhibition: parallel lines on Lineweaver-Burk; both Km and Vmax decrease proportionally; binds only ES complex

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A neonate presents on day 3 of life with poor feeding, vomiting, lethargy, and elevated plasma ammonia. Urine amino acid analysis shows NO elevated orotic acid. Plasma amino acids show no citrulline. Which enzyme deficiency is most likely?
explanation: CPS-1 deficiency: the rate-limiting enzyme of the urea cycle, located in the mitochondrial matrix, activated by N-acetylglutamate (NAG). CPS-1 deficiency causes severe neonatal hyperammonemia with NO orotic aciduria (distinguishes it from OTC deficiency, which has elevated orotic acid in urine). OTC deficiency is the most common urea cycle disorder and is X-linked recessive. In OTC deficiency, carbamoyl phosphate leaks into cytosol and enters pyrimidine synthesis, producing excess orotic acid. CPS-1 deficiency: no orotic acid, no citrulline, severe hyperammonemia.
highYield: CPS-1 deficiency: hyperammonemia WITHOUT orotic aciduria; OTC deficiency = most common, X-linked, WITH orotic aciduria

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A 6-month-old male infant presents with recurrent episodes of hyperammonemia triggered by protein feeding. Urine organic acid analysis shows elevated orotic acid. His mother has mild protein intolerance. What is the diagnosis and inheritance pattern?
explanation: OTC (Ornithine Transcarbamylase) deficiency is the MOST COMMON urea cycle disorder and the ONLY X-linked recessive one. Males are severely affected; females may have variable expression (protein intolerance). The key finding is elevated orotic acid in urine — OTC deficiency causes carbamoyl phosphate to accumulate and leak into the cytosol, entering pyrimidine synthesis and producing excess orotic acid. This distinguishes OTC from CPS-1 deficiency (no orotic acid). Citrullinemia (argininosuccinate synthase deficiency) shows elevated plasma citrulline. Arginase deficiency causes spastic diplegia, not acute neonatal crisis.
highYield: OTC deficiency: most common urea cycle disorder; X-linked recessive; elevated orotic acid in urine; males severely affected

subject: Biochemistry
stem: An infant presents with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, severe hypotonia, and hepatomegaly. Muscle biopsy shows massive glycogen accumulation in lysosomes. Which enzyme is deficient and what treatment is available?
options: Muscle glycogen phosphorylase — no effective treatment
Lysosomal acid alpha-glucosidase (acid maltase) — enzyme replacement therapy
explanation: Pompe disease (GSD type II) is caused by lysosomal acid alpha-glucosidase (acid maltase) deficiency. It is the ONLY glycogen storage disease involving LYSOSOMES. Classic infant form (Pompe's infantile): hypertrophic cardiomyopathy + hypotonia + hepatomegaly — often fatal by 2 years without treatment. Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (alglucosidase alfa). Late-onset Pompe spares the heart but causes proximal muscle weakness. McArdle disease (GSD V) is muscle phosphorylase deficiency. Von Gierke (GSD I) is glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency with severe fasting hypoglycemia, managed with uncooked cornstarch.
highYield: Pompe disease (GSD II): acid maltase deficiency; only GSD with lysosomal involvement; infant hypertrophic cardiomyopathy + hypotonia; treatment = alglucosidase alfa

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A 25-year-old man complains of painful muscle cramps during exercise with myoglobinuria. After resting briefly, he can resume exercise with less discomfort (second wind phenomenon). A forearm ischemic exercise test shows no rise in venous lactate. What is the enzyme deficiency?
explanation: McArdle disease (GSD type V) is caused by muscle glycogen phosphorylase deficiency. Features: painful muscle cramps during exercise, myoglobinuria (risk of renal failure), and no rise in venous lactate on forearm ischemic exercise test (because glycogenolysis is blocked, no lactate produced). The 'second wind' phenomenon occurs because after initial pain, fatty acid oxidation and glucose from circulation compensate. Liver function is normal (liver phosphorylase unaffected). Hers disease (GSD VI) is liver phosphorylase deficiency — mild hepatomegaly with benign course. Pompe = lysosomal, cardiomyopathy. Cori = debranching enzyme, liver + muscle.
highYield: McArdle (GSD V): muscle phosphorylase deficiency; no venous lactate rise on forearm ischemic exercise; second wind phenomenon

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A 3-year-old presents with severe fasting hypoglycemia, doll-like facies, massive hepatomegaly, and lactic acidosis. Laboratory tests show hyperuricemia and hyperlipidemia. Which enzyme deficiency explains all these findings?
options: Lysosomal acid maltase (Pompe disease, GSD II)
explanation: Von Gierke disease (GSD type I) is caused by glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency. The enzyme is present in liver and kidneys (both affected). Glucose-6-phosphate cannot be hydrolyzed to free glucose, causing: severe fasting hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis (G6P diverted to glycolysis), hyperuricemia (excess G6P to pentose phosphate pathway raises uric acid), and hyperlipidemia (excess acetyl-CoA). Hepatomegaly from massive glycogen accumulation. Doll-like facies from fat deposition. Treatment: frequent feeds and uncooked cornstarch. Kidney involvement distinguishes it from other GSDs.
highYield: Von Gierke (GSD I): glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency; severe fasting hypoglycemia + lactic acidosis + hyperuricemia + hyperlipidemia; liver + kidney

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Which enzyme is the rate-limiting step of glycolysis, and what metabolite acts as its most potent ACTIVATOR in conditions of cellular energy deficiency?
options: Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) — activated by fructose-2,6-bisphosphate and AMP
explanation: Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) is the rate-limiting (committed step) enzyme of glycolysis. It catalyzes conversion of fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate. ACTIVATORS: AMP (energy deficient state), fructose-2,6-bisphosphate (most potent activator, regulated by insulin/glucagon). INHIBITORS: ATP (energy sufficient), citrate (TCA cycle running well), low pH. Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is produced by PFK-2 (stimulated by insulin, inhibited by glucagon). The reciprocal regulation: PFK-1 activated by fructose-2,6-bisphosphate, while fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (gluconeogenesis rate-limiting enzyme) is inhibited by the same molecule.
highYield: PFK-1 = rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis; activated by AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate; inhibited by ATP and citrate

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A newborn screening test detects elevated phenylalanine. At 6 months, the untreated infant has a mousy/musty odor and is falling behind developmental milestones. Which enzyme is deficient, and what is the standard pharmacological treatment for BH4-responsive variants?
options: Phenylalanine hydroxylase — sapropterin (BH4 supplementation) for responsive variants
Homogentisic acid oxidase — dietary restriction of tyrosine
explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is caused by phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) deficiency, which normally converts phenylalanine to tyrosine (requires BH4 cofactor). Elevated phenylalanine causes intellectual disability, microcephaly, light pigmentation (low tyrosine reduces melanin), and musty/mousy odor (phenylacetic acid). Treatment: phenylalanine-restricted diet (lifelong). For BH4-responsive PKU (milder PAH mutations), sapropterin (synthetic BH4) supplementation increases residual enzyme activity. Homogentisic acid oxidase deficiency = alkaptonuria. Fumarylacetoacetase deficiency = tyrosinemia type I (treated with NTBC).
highYield: PKU = phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency; musty odor; intellectual disability; BH4-responsive variants treated with sapropterin
